main_summary.summary: Joint oral history with six participants who attended Adkin High School in Kinston, North Carolina: John Dudley, Eleanor Stewart, Charles Jarmon, Frances Suggs, Harold Suggs, and Samuel Dove. The interviewees recount their childhoods in Kinston during the 1940s and early 1950s, including work in the tobacco fields, caddying at the Kinston Country Club, the Mitchell Wooten Court housing project, and segregated retail, transit, and movie spaces. Their central testimony concerns the November 1951 Adkin High School walkout: 720 students left class in coordinated silence after the school board refused a senior-class petition for facilities equal to white Grainger High School. Five senior leaders, including Dudley and Frederick Thompson, planned the walkout in a civics class after teacher B.C. Davis left the room. Eight petitioned items were built within eighteen months. Dr. Alice Hannibal, a local activist, faced cross burnings and bomb threats for her support; senior boys stood guard at her house. Several participants describe later civil-rights work, including Dove's CORE intelligence-gathering on segregated facilities between New York and Washington that fed into the 1961 Freedom Rides, his work with SNCC writing position papers under James Forman, and Stewart's founding of a D.C. boys' choir.
key_themes: Segregated daily life in Kinston, North Carolina
Tobacco field and caddy labor for Black children
main_summary.historical_significance:
This joint interview documents one of the earliest student-organized school protests of the pre-Brown era, occurring in November 1951 — six months after the Moton High School walkout in Prince Edward County, Virginia, and three years before Brown v. Board. The participants confirm that the Kinston students were unaware of the Virginia case, indicating parallel and independent student initiative against unequal school facilities across the South. The walkout was planned entirely by twelfth-graders with no adult involvement, yielded eight built facility improvements within eighteen months, and predates the Greensboro sit-ins by more than eight years.

The interview also preserves first-person testimony on a constellation of less-documented Southern Black community institutions: Rosenwald school designations and their industrial-curriculum requirements; the Kinston Country Club caddy economy; the K-Town entertainment circuit linking Camp Lejeune, Cherry Point, Fort Bragg, and Seymour Johnson Air Force Base; and the ICC lawsuit filed by Dr. Alice Hannibal that ended Kinston's segregated bus system within a year.

chapters.title: Family Backgrounds in Kinston
chapters.summary: Each of the six interviewees describes their family origins in Kinston, North Carolina, including parents' occupations in the tobacco factory, shirt factory, mortuary work, domestic service, and small business ownership. Several grew up in Mitchell Wooten Court, the local housing project. Childhoods centered on extended family caretakers, neighborhood music traditions, and a sense — across all six — that they did not know they were poor.
related_events: Mitchell Wooten Court housing project residency

chapters.title: Race Relations and the Country Club
chapters.summary: The interviewees recall the Kinston Country Club caddy economy, where Black boys could earn sixty-five cents per two rounds. Samuel Dove describes confronting Larry Beck — who beat Jack Nicklaus in the 1957 national juniors and was the son of the club pro — after Beck called him 'boy,' ending Dove's caddying career. Participants describe the racial limits understood at the club.
related_events: Black child labor at Southern country clubs

chapters.title: Tobacco Field Labor
chapters.summary: Frances Suggs and Harold Suggs describe the tobacco-field labor cycle: pickup at 5:30 a.m., field work by men cropping leaves, women handling and looping in the barn, hanging tobacco for curing, and end-of-season barbecues. Wages were three to five dollars a day; field croppers earned ten dollars at ten hours. Participants note that tobacco-field work was one of the few integrated settings in the segregated economy — including shared drinking water from a single mason jar.
related_events: North Carolina tobacco economy in the 1940s and 1950s

chapters.title: Bus Segregation and Dr. Hannibal
chapters.summary: John Dudley and Samuel Dove recount childhood incidents on Kinston's segregated buses. Dove, age twelve, refused to give up his seat to a white woman with the retort, 'And so is toilet paper'; Black men at the back of the bus blocked the driver from forcing him to move. Dr. Alice Hannibal subsequently filed an ICC lawsuit against the bus company, which ended the city's bus system within a year.

chapters.title: Segregated Retail and Drugstore Resistance
chapters.summary: Eleanor Stewart describes waitressing at Standard Drug Store, where Black customers could not sit and had to take food out from the end of the counter. Samuel Dove recounts a 1960 action two weeks after the Greensboro sit-ins: he had a friend, Curtis Henderson, pose as a Ghanaian diplomat — invoking Ghana's recent independence — while Dove served as French interpreter, in order to integrate Standard Drug Number Two. The plan succeeded. Participants describe small acts of retaliation against five-and-dime store mistreatment.

chapters.title: Servicemen, K-Town, and Defending Sisters
chapters.summary: The participants describe Kinston as 'K-Town' — the entertainment hub between Camp Lejeune, Cherry Point, Fort Bragg, and Seymour Johnson Air Force Base. Touring Chitlin' Circuit acts including James Brown, Ruth Brown, Buddy Johnson, Arthur Prysock, and Larry Darnell played in town. Charles Jarmon's father ran the Tuxedo Club. The interviewees recount having to confront servicemen who pursued local young women, and the weekly cycle of soldiers killed in accidents returning to base.

chapters.title: Movie Theater Violence and Daily Indignities
chapters.summary: Participants describe routine racial harassment as children: white kids throwing rocks at Black children walking past the white theater to reach the Black theater; Eleanor Stewart being knocked off her bicycle, spit on, and her bike taken by white boys. The accounts establish the baseline of daily intimidation that preceded the high school walkout.

chapters.title: Planning the Adkin Walkout
chapters.summary: John Dudley narrates how the senior class of 1952, in a Wednesday civics class taught by B.C. Davis, identified eight features of an ideal school — facilities present at white Grainger High but not at Adkin. The students asked Davis to leave the room so she would not be implicated. Five seniors petitioned the school board the following Monday. The board responded that the requested items were not in the budget for ten years. The seniors then coordinated 720 students for a walkout, signaled by an announcement that 'Carolyn Colefielder has lost her red pocketbook.'

chapters.title: The Walkout and Its Aftermath
chapters.summary: On Tuesday morning, 720 students rose silently from their seats, collected pre-made placards reading 'Quality Education,' and marched down Queen Street. The principal told teachers to leave the students alone. Students stayed out until the Monday after Thanksgiving. Dr. Alice Hannibal wrote letters to the school board and faced bomb threats; senior boys took turns standing guard at her house. Within eighteen months, all eight petitioned items — including a state-of-the-art gymnasium — were built.

chapters.title: Parallel Cases and Rosenwald Schools
chapters.summary: The interviewer notes that the April 1951 Moton High School walkout in Prince Edward County, Virginia — led by Barbara Johns — had occurred seven months before the Kinston walkout, but the Kinston students were unaware of it. The five Virginia, South Carolina, Delaware, D.C., and Kansas cases consolidated as Brown v. Board; Kinston did not litigate. The participants identify Adkin as a Rosenwald school, built through the program founded by Sears chairman Julius Rosenwald at the urging of Booker T. Washington, with required industrial coursework.

chapters.title: Athletic Inequity and the Gym Fire
chapters.summary: Participants describe the 1953 Adkin state championship football team that played with hand-me-down equipment from Grainger High and 'Halloween helmet' headgear. The championship game was played at Grainger's stadium because Adkin had no facility. They recall basketball great Althea Gibson playing in Adkin's small gym while at Williston High in Wilmington. The student-built original gym burned after ashes fell on the floor during a winter pickup game — accelerating construction of the replacement gym that still stands.
related_events: Althea Gibson at Williston High School

chapters.title: Dr. Hannibal's Legacy and Civil Rights Careers
chapters.summary: Participants describe Dr. Alice Hannibal — born in New Jersey, married to a Jamaican physician trained at Fisk — as the first Black member of the Kinston city council. A municipal building in Kinston now bears her name. Samuel Dove recounts joining CORE in December 1960, conducting reconnaissance of segregated facilities between New York and Washington at James Farmer's request — intelligence that fed the April 1961 Freedom Rides. Dove later joined SNCC, wrote position papers under James Forman, organized across Southern states except Mississippi, and faced a Fayetteville police chief's death threat. Eleanor Stewart founded a D.C. boys' choir that has made international tours to Austria, England, China, and Italy.
